title: Projects
description: This project collection reflects my passion for building useful tools, exploring new technologies, and continuously improving my skills. Some projects start from curiosity, others from the need to optimize my workflow or test new ideas. In my software development work, I use AI as practical support: it helps accelerate specific phases, improve code quality, and validate new solutions quickly, always with critical review of the output.
load_preview_tooltip: The preview loads only on click to avoid unnecessary API calls.

exercises.subtitle: Selected GitHub starred repositories I use as practical exercises and learning references.

codexpane.title: CodexPane
codexpane.description: Focused technical reference workspace that turns scattered docs, examples, cheatsheets, and playgrounds into one fast study flow.

introduction.title: CodexPane
description: CodexPane started from a practical workflow issue: while studying and developing, I was losing time switching between documentation tabs, snippets, examples, and small experiments. I built it as a focused workspace for moving from technical question to usable implementation faster.

The project combines curated documentation, practical examples, cheatsheets, client-side search, and interactive playgrounds in one flow. Its value is not the number of features, but the way those pieces reduce friction during study and development.

search_mechanics: Content is indexed client-side with MiniSearch, so lookups stay fast without relying on external search services.
Queries run across section titles and text blocks, returning relevance-ordered results that are easy to scan.
Data is structured to balance search speed, result quality, and long-term maintainability.
The search UX is designed to minimize time between a technical question and concrete implementation.

react.description: React structures the workspace into reusable areas for documentation, examples, playgrounds, and navigation, keeping the experience modular as content grows.

javascript.description: JavaScript coordinates search behavior, route state, content composition, and interactive examples without adding unnecessary backend dependency.

mui.description: MUI provides the accessible component baseline for tabs, panels, controls, and readable content surfaces, reducing custom UI work while keeping consistency.

tailwindcss.description: Tailwind CSS is used selectively for layout and spacing refinements where the documentation surfaces needed more precise control than component defaults.

minisearch.description: MiniSearch powers local full-text lookup, letting users search curated content quickly without a server-side search service.

caddy.description: Caddy handles HTTPS and reverse-proxy deployment so the tool can be published reliably with lean operational configuration.

github.description: GitHub keeps documentation changes, release history, and automation traceable as the content model evolves.

webstorm.description: WebStorm supports refactoring across content config, route definitions, and UI components, helping keep the project navigable.

css.description: CSS covers targeted visual details for examples and playground areas where utility classes or MUI tokens are not enough.

codex.description: Codex/ChatGPT supported content-structure decisions, implementation iteration, and review, with manual validation before adopting outputs.

difficulties_faced: Designing a documentation workspace that stayed faster than switching between browser tabs, snippets, and separate reference tools.
Building client-side search that could index technical content while keeping results quick, relevant, and easy to scan.
Balancing feature depth with focus, so playgrounds and cheatsheets added value without turning the app into a cluttered toolbox.
Keeping documentation content structured enough to scale across languages, frameworks, sections, and subsections.
Defining routing and naming conventions that made topic navigation predictable as the content model grew.
Maintaining visual consistency across documentation, examples, and interactive areas while still allowing each content type to breathe.
lessons_learned: A technical reference tool is useful only when it reduces lookup friction; fast search and clean content structure matter more than feature count.
Client-side search works well when the content model is designed for indexing from the beginning.
Documentation products need editorial discipline: naming, hierarchy, and examples must evolve together.
Interactive examples are strongest when they support the reading flow instead of becoming a separate destination.

v1.title: v1.0.0
content: Release the first usable workspace with core documentation search, basic examples, and the initial navigation model.
Publish the app on VPS infrastructure behind HTTPS to make the tool accessible outside local development.
Introduce automated GitHub-based release flow with versioned deploys to reduce manual publishing steps.

v2.title: v2.0.0
content: Expand documentation categories while preserving predictable section/subsection relationships.
Improve playground consistency so examples feel like part of the reading flow rather than separate widgets.
Strengthen search by indexing deeper content and highlighting matched text inside results.

v3.title: v3.0.0
content: Add contributor guidance so new content can follow the same naming, structure, and quality expectations.
Credit contributors directly on related documents and sections to make authorship visible.

v4.title: v4.0.0
content: Add cheatsheet-style pages with copy/download actions for quick technical recall.
Explore a documentation-grounded assistant that helps users find relevant sections without replacing curated content.

ecommerce-rest-api.title: E-commerce REST API
ecommerce-rest-api.description: Full-stack e-commerce demo built to exercise product flows, session auth, documented APIs, and versioned VPS deployment under real constraints.

introduction.title: E-commerce REST API
description: This project was built to make a portfolio e-commerce demo feel closer to a real engineering system than a static storefront: catalog browsing, session-based authentication, cart and checkout flows, API documentation, and deployment all had to work together.

The core constraint was publishing frontend and backend under one public domain while keeping backend traffic proxied through `/api`, preserving a clean user experience and a safer operational boundary.

react.description: React drives the catalog, cart, auth, and checkout screens with reusable UI patterns that keep user flows easy to extend.

vite.description: Vite keeps frontend iteration fast while testing product-flow changes across catalog, cart, and authentication screens.

react-router.description: React Router separates public, auth, cart, checkout, and documentation views while keeping navigation predictable in the SPA.

javascript.description: JavaScript keeps the demo stack consistent across frontend and Express backend, reducing context switching while building full-stack flows.

mui.description: MUI provides accessible components for forms, tables, dialogs, and product surfaces, helping the demo feel inspectable rather than throwaway.

tailwindcss.description: Tailwind CSS is used sparingly for layout refinements where quick utility styling was more efficient than custom component variants.

nodejs.description: Node.js runs the API layer and operational scripts, tying backend behavior, deployment checks, and demo reset flows into one runtime.

express.description: Express structures REST endpoints for products, carts, auth, checkout, and operational health with clear middleware boundaries.

passport.description: Passport keeps local and optional Google OAuth strategies modular, so authentication can evolve without rewriting protected routes.

express-session.description: express-session persists authenticated user state for protected cart/order flows, matching the demo goal of realistic session behavior.

postgresql.description: PostgreSQL models users, products, carts, and orders relationally, making data constraints explicit and easier to inspect.

caddy.description: Caddy publishes frontend and API through one HTTPS domain while keeping backend services reachable only through the intended proxy path.

github.description: GitHub tracks code, deploy workflow changes, and release history across frontend and backend parts of the demo.

github-actions.description: GitHub Actions automates build, deploy, and controlled activation steps so VPS releases are repeatable instead of manual.

swagger-openapi.description: Swagger/OpenAPI makes endpoints, payloads, and auth behavior visible to reviewers without requiring source-code reading first.

webstorm.description: WebStorm supports refactoring and navigation across the full-stack demo, especially shared naming across routes, API handlers, and docs.

codex.description: Codex/ChatGPT supported deployment scripting and documentation passes, with manual validation of operational and architecture choices.

difficulties_faced: Designing robust session-based authentication (local login + optional Google OAuth) while keeping the user flow consistent.
Handling frontend/API routing through a single domain proxy without exposing backend services directly.
Building a reliable VPS deployment pipeline with versioned releases and controlled activation.
Managing demo database reset on backend deploy without compromising runtime stability and functional checks.
Documenting APIs and payloads clearly to keep the project immediately verifiable for recruiters and developers.
lessons_learned: A demo project becomes more credible when it includes operational concerns such as deploy, reset flows, and API documentation.
Session-based authentication affects both backend design and frontend UX, so it needs to be treated as a full flow rather than a single endpoint.
Keeping frontend and backend under one domain simplifies the public experience, but requires disciplined proxy and routing boundaries.
Clear Swagger/OpenAPI documentation turns backend behavior into something inspectable instead of hidden implementation detail.

v1.title: v1.0.0
content: Ship the monorepo foundation with React/Vite frontend, Express backend, and initial PostgreSQL schema.
Implement core product flows: catalog, cart, checkout, local authentication, and protected order behavior.
Publish through VPS/Caddy with backend access constrained to the `/api` proxy path.

v2.title: v2.0.0
content: Harden authentication and session flows, including optional Google OAuth and clearer protected-route behavior.
Improve API validation, error responses, and Swagger coverage so payload behavior is easier to verify.
Add operational visibility through health checks, logs, and deploy troubleshooting notes.

v3.title: v3.0.0
content: Add end-to-end tests for critical flows such as auth, cart, checkout, and demo reset behavior.
Strengthen contributor documentation around local setup, API contracts, and deployment operations.
Introduce baseline API performance metrics to make response-time regressions visible.

artap.title: Artap
artap.description: Full-stack website for the ART.AP cultural association, built around bilingual public content, events, news, blog, gallery, and maintainable admin workflows.

introduction.title: Artap
description: Artap is the real public website for the ART.AP cultural association in Villa del Conte, created to present cultural activities, exhibitions, events, news, blog articles, gallery albums, and contact flows in a bilingual experience.

The strongest technical focus is the admin area: authenticated editors can manage content, publish drafts, upload media, and build event pages through a visual canvas with modular widgets, page settings, poster handling, and IT/EN content support.

lessons_learned: A real association website needs editorial workflows as much as public pages: drafts, publishing, media reuse, and bilingual content have to stay practical for non-technical usage.
A visual event builder becomes manageable only when layout, widget data, localized copy, and shared metadata are separated clearly.
Protected admin APIs, shared schemas, and predictable upload paths reduce the risk of content drifting between frontend, backend, and production storage.

AI is most useful as a development accelerator for frontend iteration, repetitive implementation passes, translation-assisted drafts, and review, while product and architecture choices still need manual validation.

react.description: React structures the public site and admin surfaces into reusable pages, widgets, cards, dialogs, and editor flows for a bilingual cultural platform.

vite.description: Vite keeps frontend development fast while iterating on public pages, admin editors, and the event-builder canvas.

react-router.description: React Router manages localized public routes, detail pages, admin entry points, and dedicated event/blog editor routes.

typescript.description: TypeScript protects shared contracts across web, API, and the shared package, reducing drift while content models evolve.

mui.description: MUI provides accessible primitives for admin forms, dialogs, buttons, cards, and dense editing panels where consistency matters.

tailwindcss.description: Tailwind CSS supports targeted layout and styling refinements alongside the MUI component layer.

tanstack-query.description: TanStack Query coordinates public and admin data loading, mutations, invalidation, and draft refresh behavior.

i18next.description: react-i18next centralizes bilingual IT/EN copy for public pages, admin controls, widget labels, and content-management feedback.

nodejs.description: Node.js runs the API and operational tooling behind content publishing, uploads, translation helpers, and deployment checks.

fastify.description: Fastify structures public reads and protected admin endpoints for posts, events, gallery albums, media assets, uploads, settings, and contact.

mongodb.description: MongoDB, via Mongoose models, stores localized content records, event documents, gallery albums, media metadata, and editorial status.

zod.description: Zod validates shared event-builder documents and API payloads so widget content fails predictably before reaching production data.

caddy.description: Caddy publishes the full-stack site on VPS with HTTPS and reverse-proxy routing between frontend, API, and uploaded assets.

github.description: GitHub keeps the monorepo history, deployment templates, workflow updates, and release evolution traceable.

github-actions.description: GitHub Actions supports CI and manual production deploy flows with build, typecheck, test, and VPS release steps.

webstorm.description: WebStorm supports navigation and refactoring across a large monorepo with web, API, shared schemas, and deployment files.

codex.description: Codex/ChatGPT helped accelerate frontend iteration, slower implementation passes, copy refinement, and technical review without replacing manual validation.

difficulties_faced: Turning a cultural association website into a maintainable editorial system instead of a static brochure.
Designing an admin panel where news, blog posts, events, gallery albums, uploads, drafts, and publish actions stay coherent.
Building the event canvas editor with widget library, drag/resize behavior, page settings, media selection, and bilingual content without making the UI fragile.
Keeping frontend, Fastify APIs, MongoDB/Mongoose models, and shared Zod contracts aligned across localized content records.
Managing upload paths, media-library reuse, and production storage rules so public images remain stable after deployment.
Using AI to speed up repetitive and slow development phases while still reviewing architecture, accessibility, and content quality manually.

v1.title: v1.0.0
content: Ship the public bilingual association site with routes for home, about, events, news, blog, gallery, contact, and legal pages.
Introduce Auth0-protected admin access and the first CMS flows for creating, editing, saving drafts, and publishing content.
Publish the full-stack foundation through VPS/Caddy with frontend and API configured for production.

v2.title: v2.0.0
content: Expand event authoring into a dedicated visual builder with widget library, grid canvas, poster handling, and page settings.
Add reusable media-library workflows for event posters, event widgets, blog/news covers, and gallery album images.
Align gallery, news, blog, and event editorial flows around bilingual drafts, linked content, and clearer publish actions.

v3.title: v3.0.0
content: Harden admin-session behavior, upload storage contracts, protected API validation, and deployment checks.
Broaden test coverage around event builder, content publishing, API contracts, and media handling.
Refine editorial maintenance with better documentation, stable selectors, and smaller builder modules.

portfolio.title: Portfolio
portfolio.description: Personal portfolio built to connect experience, projects, CV, release history, and technical writing in one maintainable bilingual showcase.

introduction.title: Portfolio
description: I built this portfolio to make my work understandable at different depths: quick project cards for scanning, technical detail pages for deeper review, CV/contact flows for professional follow-up, and release notes that show how the site evolves over time.

The main challenge was keeping personal content, bilingual copy, routing, breadcrumbs, project metadata, static snapshots, and release artifacts coherent without turning a personal site into a fragile custom CMS.

lessons_learned: A portfolio is stronger when it behaves like a maintained product, not just a static presentation page.
Centralizing route, breadcrumb, and project metadata reduces drift between navigation and content.
Writing project copy in two languages forces clearer thinking about what each project actually proves.
Automated release notes and static snapshots make even a personal site easier to trust and maintain.

react.description: React structures the portfolio into route-level pages and reusable sections, making it easier to evolve projects, CV, and layout independently.

javascript.description: JavaScript coordinates routing models, project view models, release-note rendering, and UI state while keeping the app lightweight.

mui.description: MUI provides the accessible layout and interaction baseline for navigation, cards, modals, lists, and theme-aware surfaces.

caddy.description: Caddy publishes the portfolio with HTTPS and reverse-proxy simplicity, keeping deploy configuration lean for a personal VPS setup.

github.description: GitHub stores source history, release artifacts, and automation changes so the public site evolution stays traceable.

webstorm.description: WebStorm supports navigation and refactoring across routes, translations, project data, and layout components.

codex.description: Codex/ChatGPT is used as a technical collaborator for documentation, refactors, and release-note workflows, with human review before changes land.

difficulties_faced: Keeping navigation, routes, breadcrumbs, and project detail pages aligned from a single source of truth.
Maintaining IT/EN content parity while writing project copy that feels natural in both languages.
Balancing visual consistency with enough flexibility for pages such as CV, About, Projects, and Release Notes.
Creating a breadcrumb experience with terminal-style navigation while preserving predictable browser routing.
Automating release notes and static data snapshots without adding browser runtime dependencies.

v1.title: v1.0.0
content: Establish the portfolio foundation with modular React pages, shared layout, and primary route structure.
Add IT/EN localization and theme support early so content and UI conventions can scale together.
Publish the first VPS-backed version with a repeatable release path instead of manual file updates.

v2.title: v2.0.0
content: Consolidate versioning and release artifacts so site updates are easier to explain and audit.
Expand project pages with richer metadata, technical context, and clearer preview/live/GitHub actions.
Refine navigation and visual consistency as the site grows beyond the first page set.

v3.title: v3.0.0
content: Introduce SEO meta management and lazy-loaded sections to improve discoverability and perceived performance.
Add Contact and Certifications areas with route, navigation, and translation coverage kept in sync.
Move release notes and static project data toward generated snapshots instead of runtime browser fetches.

v4.title: v4.0.0
content: Add Lighthouse score snapshots to project pages so quality can be compared across releases.
Show Performance, Accessibility, Best Practices, and SEO breakdowns in a reviewer-friendly format.
Use score history to make future portfolio improvements measurable rather than purely visual.

logra.title: Logra
logra.description: Personal gaming library app shaped around backlog decisions, progress tracking, release dates, and reliable demo operations.

introduction.title: Logra
description: Logra started from a practical need: I wanted a gaming library that matched how I actually decide what to play, track progress, manage backlog state, and follow upcoming releases.

The platform combines a workflow-driven UI with IGDB-backed catalog data, controlled ingest/sync flows, demo-safe operations, and explicit data credits so the product stays usable and operationally trustworthy.

lessons_learned: A workflow-driven UI improves daily usability far more than visual polish alone.
Keeping shared contracts, API logic, and frontend components clearly separated makes fast iteration safer.
Automating deploy, seed, and environment reset flows significantly reduces manual mistakes and keeps public demos reliable.
AI is most effective when applied to focused phases (GUI design, operational scripting, bootstrap workflows) with final human technical review.

react.description: React powers reusable cards, filters, detail panels, guided tours, and settings surfaces that support frequent library interactions.

vite.description: Vite keeps frontend iteration fast while tuning dense UI states such as filters, edit mode, overlays, and dashboard widgets.

react-router.description: React Router organizes scoped navigation across dashboard, discover, library, wishlist, stats, settings, and onboarding flows.

mui.description: MUI provides theme tokens and accessible primitives for a dense operational UI that still needs to stay readable.

nodejs.description: Node.js runs API and operational scripts for seed, reset, migration, and sync tasks, keeping local and production workflows aligned.

caddy.description: Caddy handles HTTPS reverse proxy and VPS publication, simplifying production routing between frontend and API services.

github.description: GitHub keeps feature evolution, release history, and operational changes traceable across a full-stack codebase.

github-actions.description: GitHub Actions automates deploys and controlled maintenance tasks such as demo resets, release notes, and data sync operations.

webstorm.description: WebStorm supports debugging and refactoring across web, API, shared contracts, and operational scripts.

codex.description: Codex/ChatGPT accelerates GUI iteration, scripts, and operational procedures while final architecture decisions remain manually reviewed.

typescript.description: TypeScript protects shared contracts across web, API, and packages, reducing payload drift during fast feature work.

fastify.description: Fastify structures modular API routes, session auth, validation, and low-overhead request handling for library operations.

i18next.description: react-i18next centralizes IT/EN copy for onboarding, feedback, settings, and dynamic game-library states.

mongodb.description: MongoDB stores users, sessions, gaming collections, and operational metadata with migration/index flows for long-term maintenance.

igdb.description: IGDB supplies catalog metadata through controlled ingest, discover fallback, and user-triggered materialization flows.

zod.description: Zod validates runtime contracts between frontend and API so settings, onboarding, and library payloads fail predictably.

tanstack-query.description: TanStack Query coordinates client caching, mutations, and invalidation so library and wishlist updates stay responsive.

framer-motion.description: Framer Motion handles card, overlay, and detail transitions while preserving control in dense edit and guided-tour flows.

difficulties_faced: Stabilizing multi-step guided tours (overlay, spotlight, target fallback) without blocking critical guided interactions.
Keeping frontend and API aligned on shared contracts (settings, onboarding, preferences) during fast iteration cycles.
Managing demo seed/reset flows, migrations, and legacy collection cleanup without impacting public demo reliability.
Balancing dense card information with a fast UX, especially in edit mode, filters, and quick-action flows.
Maintaining UX quality and operational stability across local environments, CI/CD, and versioned VPS deployments.

v1.title: v1.0.0
content: Release the core product with demo login, game-library management, status/progress tracking, favorites, and multi-section navigation.
Introduce profile and appearance settings plus scope release gating so future feature areas can evolve modularly.
Create operational foundations for seed, bootstrap, and shared demo lifecycle management.

v2.title: v2.0.0
content: Expand onboarding with multi-page guided tours and contextual card/edit-mode tours to reduce adoption friction.
Deepen IGDB integration with ingest sync, data segmentation, discover fallback, and clearer catalog quality controls.
Harden production operations with safer bootstrap credentials, updated docs, and more reliable release procedures.

v3.title: v3.0.0
content: Add contextual feedback and bug reporting so page-level issues arrive with better traceability.
Introduce new stats widgets that turn library data into more useful personal insight.
Add per-widget configuration for shown data, detail level, and dedicated display controls.
